summary: Obfuscated bulk-email/SMS spam framework with OAuth inbox-phishing helper
details: nolimit-x is shipped as 'Advanced email sender' but its entire runtime is hidden in a `.ad/` directory containing 40+ files packed with obfuscator.io (string-array, RC4 decoder, self-defending and debug-protection transforms). Deobfuscation reveals offensive infrastructure rather than a normal mail library: raw-SMTP send engine, SMTP smuggling, envelope splitting, fake Message-ID generation, on-the-fly DKIM key generation for arbitrary domains, TLS-fingerprint spoofing, attachment/MIME tricks and explicit anti-filter 'turbo' / 'stealth' modes. The `nolimit auth microsoft` CLI subcommand (.ad/x12.js) drives an OAuth 2.0 device-code flow using the well-known Microsoft Office first-party client_id `9e5f94bc-e8a4-4e73-b8be-63364c29d753` (a pre-consented client commonly abused by phishing kits) and requests `Mail.Read Contacts.Read People.Read offline_access` against Microsoft Graph; the `extract` subcommand then walks `/me/contacts`, `/me/people`, `/me/mailFolders/inbox/messages` and `/me/mailFolders/sentitems/messages` to harvest the consenting victim's mailbox and address book. The `nolimit activate` flow POSTs HWID, hostname and platform to an author-controlled license server and stamps the resulting license_key into every outbound mail job. Native send engines for darwin/linux/win32 are pulled via `optionalDependencies` from the publisher's own `@nolimit-x/*` scope at non-exact `^1.0.x` ranges. Installing this package places a packaged spam/phishing toolkit and OAuth inbox-stealer on the developer's machine; downstream developers and any organisation whose inboxes are coerced through the device-code prompt are the victims.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
